explanation: This is the most common and direct interpretation. It states that if the autopilot is currently in the MANEUVER state and both the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, then it is guaranteed that the system will eventually transition to the STANDBY state.

This provides a more precise description of the state transition. It states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and the conditions are met, it must leave the MANEUVER state and remain out of it until the STANDBY state is eventually reached, ensuring a clean transition.

This states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and the conditions are met, then starting from the next time step, it is guaranteed that the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state.
output_LTL: G ( (state_is_MANEUVER && standby && good) -> X F state_is_STANDBY )

explanation: This explicitly captures the 'change from' aspect by stating that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and the conditions are met, then it is guaranteed that the autopilot will eventually be in the STANDBY state and no longer in the MANEUVER state.
This interpretation emphasizes that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER, then the conditions (pilot in control and good sensor data) must hold continuously until the STANDBY state is eventually reached.

This is a strong interpretation implying an immediate transition. It states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and the conditions are met, then in the very next time step, the autopilot must be in the STANDBY state.

This is a reordering of the antecedent conditions from the primary interpretation, logically equivalent but structurally different. It states that if all three conditions (pilot in control, good sensor data, and MANEUVER state) are true, the system will eventually enter STANDBY.

This is logically equivalent to the first interpretation due to implication rules (A -> (B -> C) is equivalent to (A && B) -> C). It emphasizes that being in MANEUVER implies that if the conditions are met, STANDBY will eventually be reached.

This implies an immediate transition, stating that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and conditions are met, then it must be in STANDBY at the current time (i.e., immediately). Note that G[0,1] P is equivalent to P based on the provided definition.

This states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and the conditions are met, then it is guaranteed that it will eventually be in STANDBY AND it will also eventually not be in MANEUVER, explicitly stating both outcomes of a change.
This states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and conditions are met, then it must either be in STANDBY immediately, or starting from the next step, it will eventually be in STANDBY.
output_LTL: G ( (state_is_MANEUVER && standby && good) -> (state_is_STANDBY || X F state_is_STANDBY) )

explanation: This states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and conditions are met, then eventually it will be in STANDBY and the pilot will be in control and sensor data will be good, implying the conditions might also hold at the destination.

explanation: This states that if the autopilot is in MANEUVER and conditions are met, then eventually it will be in STANDBY, and also eventually it will be in STANDBY and no longer in MANEUVER. This is somewhat redundant but emphasizes both aspects of the transition.
